title: Trauma and the Body's Voice: Finding and Using Your Authentic Expression
excerpt: Discover how trauma silences the body’s authentic voice and learn somatic strategies to reclaim genuine self-expression and healing.
body:
Understanding Trauma as a Somatic Experience

Trauma is often misunderstood as solely a psychological or emotional event. However, decades of research show that trauma is fundamentally stored in the body. Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneer in trauma research, famously stated, “Trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you.” This internalization manifests physically—in muscle tension, chronic pain, altered breathing patterns, and even disruptions in our nervous system regulation.

When the body experiences overwhelming threat, it reacts automatically through the autonomic nervous system (ANS). Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory elucidates how these nervous system responses—freeze, fight, or flight—impact our ability to connect with both ourselves and others. Trauma effectively silences the body’s natural rhythms and its authentic voice, leaving survivors disconnected from their own somatic signals.

The Body’s Voice: What Is Authentic Expression?

Authentic expression isn’t just about words or conscious thoughts. It’s the full-bodied communication of who we are—our emotions, needs, boundaries, and truths—expressed through posture, breath, tone, and movement. Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy emphasizes that trauma survivors often lose this expression because their bodies become stuck in defensive postures or dissociation.

Our authentic self-expression is the language of the body that tells our story without distortion. When trauma suppresses this voice, individuals might feel numb, frozen, or experience emotional dysregulation. The challenge is to rediscover and trust the body’s signals, which can feel unfamiliar or even frightening due to past overwhelm.

Why Traditional Talk Therapy May Not Reach the Body’s Voice

Many people feel stuck after years of talk therapy because verbal processing alone often bypasses the body’s trauma imprints. Francine Shapiro’s Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) and Pat Ogden’s sensorimotor approaches highlight the limits of purely cognitive interventions. Trauma memories reside in implicit, nonverbal systems—our muscles, organs, and autonomic responses—not just in conscious memory.

Dan Siegel underscores the importance of integrating body and mind (‘mindsight’) for healing. Without somatic engagement, clients might intellectually understand their trauma but remain disconnected from their felt experience, blocking authentic expression.

Finding Your Body’s Voice: Practical Somatic Strategies

Reconnecting with your body’s authentic expression requires intentional, body-based practices that honor the nervous system’s current state. Here are evidence-informed approaches to begin this work:

  • Tracking Sensations: Begin by noticing subtle bodily sensations without judgment. Pat Ogden teaches that this mindful awareness builds tolerance and trust in somatic signals.
  • Breath Regulation: Controlled, slow breathing activates the parasympathetic nervous system, promoting safety and calm. Deb Dana’s work on Polyvagal-informed regulation offers practical breath exercises.
  • Movement and Posture: Gentle movement or yoga can release trapped energy and shift defensive postures. Peter Levine’s Somatic Experiencing emphasizes pendulation—moving between states of tension and relaxation.
  • Expressive Arts: Using voice, dance, or drawing can help bypass verbal defenses and access deeper feelings and narratives stored in the body.
  • Grounding Techniques: Engage your senses—feel your feet on the ground, notice textures, or hear ambient sounds—to anchor yourself in the present moment.

Using Your Authentic Expression for Healing and Empowerment

Reclaiming your body’s voice is not just about feeling better physically; it’s about restoring agency and connection. Gabor Maté stresses that trauma disconnects us from our authentic self, leading to addictive or self-destructive patterns. When the body’s voice is reclaimed, survivors can set boundaries, communicate needs clearly, and experience deeper relational intimacy.

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model complements somatic approaches by helping individuals understand and integrate different parts of themselves, including those holding trauma. Authentic expression becomes a bridge between internal parts and external relationships, fostering wholeness.

Challenges and Common Pitfalls in Somatic Healing

Healing trauma somatically isn’t linear or easy. Many survivors experience re-traumatization if they push too fast or ignore the nervous system’s cues. It’s critical to pace yourself and seek support from trauma-informed practitioners when needed.

Also, some may struggle with shame or fear around their bodily reactions. Recognizing that these are survival adaptations—not personal weaknesses—is key. As Bessel van der Kolk notes, “The body keeps the score,” but with compassionate awareness, it also holds the key to recovery.

The Future of Trauma Healing: Integrating Body and Mind

The neuroscience of trauma is clear: healing demands more than talking. It requires a synthesis of body, brain, and relational connection. Integrating somatic methods with psychological insight offers a fuller path to authentic self-expression and resilience.

Emerging research supports combining approaches like EMDR, sensorimotor therapy, and Polyvagal-informed interventions for lasting change. As our understanding deepens, so does the hope that trauma survivors can truly hear, honor, and use their body’s voice.

“Healing trauma means learning to listen deeply to the body’s voice—the language that has been silenced by survival.” – Bessel van der Kolk

faqs.q: Why does trauma affect the body’s ability to express itself?
faqs.a: Trauma triggers survival responses in the nervous system that can freeze, numb, or hyperactivate the body, disrupting natural expression and connection to internal signals.

faqs.q: How can somatic practices help with trauma recovery?
faqs.a: Somatic practices re-regulate the nervous system, build tolerance to bodily sensations, and restore communication between mind and body, enabling authentic expression and healing.

faqs.q: Is talk therapy enough to heal trauma?
faqs.a: While talk therapy is valuable, trauma is often stored nonverbally in the body. Integrating somatic approaches addresses these implicit memories, facilitating deeper healing.

faqs.q: Can anyone learn to find their authentic bodily voice after trauma?
faqs.a: Yes. With patience, safety, and appropriate guidance, individuals can reconnect with and trust their body’s signals, leading to restored authentic self-expression.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma is stored in the body and offers insights into somatic healing.

affiliateProducts.title: Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: A practical guide to Somatic Experiencing, focusing on releasing trauma through body awareness and movement.

affiliateProducts.title: Polyvagal Exercises for Safety and Connection by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: Provides simple, effective exercises to regulate the nervous system and promote safety through the Polyvagal lens.
